text.title: Onboarding Email Framework
text.summary: Segment taxonomy, trigger types, 8-email sequence structure, and escalation ladder for stuck users.

section.title: User Segments

rule.statement: Segment users before writing any email — each segment receives a different path with different goals.
rule.rationale: Generic sequences treat activated users and inactive users identically, wasting effort and triggering unsubscribes from engaged users.

list.item: New + Active: engaged, hitting milestones → accelerate to activation
New + Stuck: started but stopped → remove blockers
New + Inactive: signed up, no activity → re-engage
Activated: hit aha moment → path to conversion
At-risk: dropping engagement → win back

section.p: Segment dimensions: signup source, use case, company size, actual behavior.

rule.statement: Use behavior-triggered emails as the primary mechanism; supplement with time-based emails only for users with no trackable actions.
rule.rationale: Behavioral triggers outperform time-based sequences by 15-40% in activation rate because they reach users at the exact moment of need.

list.item: Time trigger: days since signup (e.g., Day 3 feature highlight)
Action trigger: user completed step X (e.g., "You created a project!")
Inaction trigger: step X NOT completed within Y days (e.g., "Did you get stuck?")
Hybrid: time + behavior condition (e.g., Day 3 AND not activated)

rule.statement: Map each email to a single trigger event and a single goal before writing copy.
rule.rationale: Emails with mixed goals produce mixed results; the sequence design determines effectiveness more than individual copy quality.

list.item: 1. Signup → Welcome: set expectations, prompt first action
2. Didn't complete action → Gentle nudge: remove blocker
3. Completed first action → Celebration + next step: build momentum
4. Day 3 if not activated → Feature education: show value
5. Completed activation → Path to power use: deepen engagement
6. Day 7 trial → Mid-point check: offer help
7. High usage → Social proof + upgrade: convert
8. Low usage → Re-engagement: rescue

section.title: Escalation Ladder for Stuck Users

rule.statement: Build a 6-level escalation path for users who take no action after signup; cap escalation frequency at 1 email per day maximum.
rule.rationale: Without escalation, stuck users silently churn. Without a frequency cap, users unsubscribe — hurting overall deliverability.

list.item: Level 1 — No action, Day 2: helpful email
Level 2 — No action, Day 5: different angle email
Level 3 — No action, Day 7: video tutorial link
Level 4 — No action, Day 10: personal outreach offer
Level 5 — No action, Day 12: extension or special offer
Level 6 — Trial ends: exit survey

section.note: The Day 10 personal outreach email scales to approximately 200 new signups/month without a dedicated success team. Above that threshold, route to a Slack notification for a human to review rather than fully automated send.
